← all tutorials

Sync vs Async, FLP & Partial Synchrony — interactive 📖 SYNC_VS_ASYNC.md 📄 sync_vs_async.py

📖 Full guide  ·  📄 Ground-truth source — every panel below recomputes the same model in JS.
Drag Δ and watch the synchronous failure detector declare a crashed node dead after exactly 2Δ of silence. Then flip to the async world, where a slow node is indistinguishable from a dead one — the crack that FLP drives open. Finally watch Raft's 150-300ms timeout ride the partial-synchrony middle ground. All numbers are recomputed live in JS from the identical formulas as sync_vs_async.py.

① Synchronous model — a crashed node is declared dead after exactly 2Δ of silence

What to do: drag Δ (the one-way delay bound). n0 pings n2 every 1.0s; n2 crashes at t=2.5s. Each ping's reply must arrive within 2Δ. The first ping whose reply window expires with no reply → n2 declared DEAD.
ping interval = 1.0s · crash @ t=2.5s · matches sync_vs_async.py Section A
ping (n0→n2) reply (n2→n0), alive no reply → DEAD @ 2Δ crash @ 2.5s
The synchronous superpower: the bound is a hard promise, so silence is proof of death — detection is complete (every crash caught) and accurate (zero false positives). detection_time = 2Δ.

② Asynchronous model — 'slow' is indistinguishable from 'crashed'

What to do: toggle the hidden truth of n2 (alive-but-slow vs crashed). Then pick a timeout T. From n0's viewpoint the two worlds look identical until T fires — and whatever T you pick, it is wrong in one world.
probe (n0→n2) @ t=0 reply (only if alive + arrives) timeout fires @ T
Core of FLP's seed: there is no correct failure detector in the pure async model. For any T the adversary sets delay = T+ε and forces a false 'dead' verdict on a live node.

③ FLP — the bivalent chain that never decides

What to do: step through the adversary's strategy. p0 proposes 0, p1 proposes 1. Whichever message is delivered first decides the outcome. The adversary delays both forever, so the state stays bivalent (both 0 and 1 reachable) — termination is violated.
p0 → p1 : value 0 (m0) p1 → p0 : value 1 (m1) adversary: delay both bivalent (undecided)

④ Partial synchrony — 'eventually synchronous' (DLS 1988)

What to do: drag GST. Before GST, leader heartbeats get delayed past the timeout → leaders churn. After GST, delays fall within Δ → a leader survives and consensus proceeds. The protocol never needed to know GST.
Δ_after = 1.0s · matches sync_vs_async.py Section D
leader deposed (delay > timeout) leader survives (delay ≤ Δ) <<< GST >>>

⑤ Timeout trade-off — false elections vs failover latency (Raft 150-300ms)

What to do: drag the election timeout T. Model: heartbeat delay ~ Exponential(μ=50ms), so P(false election) = e^(−T/μ). Too short → constant leader churn; too long → slow failover. Raft's sweet spot is 150-300ms.
μ = 50ms · matches sync_vs_async.py Section E
check: … JS recompute matches sync_vs_async.py: detection_time = 2Δ = 2.0s at Δ=1.0 (Section A gold).
